speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We had mentioned that you had relatives in the United States. Did you ever communicate with them-- let them know what was going on? And can you tell us maybe about some of the events you might have told them about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did have a lot of-- United-- we still have a lot of-- in the United States. I do have. For instance, my mother brother-- one of the brothers-- he immigrated to the United States 1900. He ran away from school. He couldn't take it. They used to call him [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, he couldn't take it. So he ran away. And he knew he had his great grandmother's sister. They came over here before civil war yet. So he ran away to the United States. These days he didn't need to have an affidavit or things like that-- just got a ticket, and sometime, even without a ticket, they came over here. So that's why he came over. He was only 14, 15 years old he came over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emil Mendler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived-- first he lived in New York. Then he, when he got married, my aunt come from Uniontown. Then they moved to Latrobe. And that's where I end up at, in Latrobe--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --after I found him. I didn't know he lived in Latrobe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and your family kept in touch with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We kept in touch with him, yes, always. That's why I'm one of the fortunate men. You know, I have the pictures from my family, because we used to send him pictures and things like this-- correspond always with him. And that's why I have some of the pictures from previous years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, my father had also relatives here in New York, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but we didn't keep too much correspondence with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I imagine during your lifetime, your parents, your mother, would write to your uncle about good times and bad times. And any particular incidents that stay in your mind that you told them about, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew what was happening, what was coming when my father relation came to Poland from Germany-- when they were chased out from Germany. I won't say chased out. They had a chance to get out-- came to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father wrote to his uncle. This is my grandfather brother. They live in New York. They still use the name Reibeisen.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I changed my name, but I'll tell you after a while why. And my father begged him. They were pretty well off-- then already they were very well off, as a matter of fact-- not him, but his children-- very well off.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father begged him to save somebody from the farm-- my brother for instance. You know, my father want to send my brother to the United States. He was already 16 years old, something like that. And no response-- very, very few. That's why I don't have much contact with him, you know? Yeah. How did you know that there was-- how did your father know that there was danger coming? Were there any incidents in Nowy Targ?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew because they come from Germany. So we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you ever in personal danger before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were, yes. For instance, in 1933, we have a little pogrom in our town, which was nothing unusual, because some towns, they always have pogroms-- not always, but you heard about it-- I'm sure, here in the United States, too. I remember this was during the summertime, because we were on vacation out of school. And me and my brother-- I was just a little fellow. I must have been about 8 years old-- something like that-- 7 years old, maybe. We were playing in the park. The park was not too far from my home-- from my father's factory, actually. In 1933, this was in July, I believe, that little program-- what started, the farmers start to complain to the government they didn't get enough pay for the product they brought to the-- to the market, and so forth on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was the end? They're always Jews. Then they start to break the windows in Jewish homes, beat-- beat up the Jewish people, burn the houses, you know, or something. So my father sent one of our helpers, one of his helpers to the park-- not to come home. Stay in the park. And he was our-- I ride with us-- me and my brother. And he won't let us to come home till everything quiet down. Was anyone killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody was killed, but it was-- but a lot of damage was done to our Jewish homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to-- how about the Jewish businesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's what I said. They were looted--

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it-- how did you know it was safe to come home?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the police came in from different towns, quiet down. They chased the farmers back to the-- to the villages. Fortunately, you know, we were always Jewish. People were always scapegoats-- which we were. First thing-- something-- always-- we were always blamed for everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And who actually was doing these attacks? The-- who was leading the pogrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not so much the farmers, it's the anti-Semite, the Andechs, what we used to call them. They were the one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain what they're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Andechs-- they were people-- they were anti-Semite against the Jewish people. They used to-- for instance, they used to picket our stores-- Jewish businesses-- don't buy by the Jews. Jews are Communist. They are Communist. They-- they cause all kind of-- they wouldn't allow the Polish people to shop at Jewish businesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were these people from your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were from our town, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And these people continued to live in the town after the Germans came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they cooperate with the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the head of the-- as a matter-- with the Germans? From the beginning, they were very well, you know. But after a while, the Germans didn't need them, you know, as far as that.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, the head of the-- the anti-Semite organization, he was a guy-- he delivered me. You know, there wasn't too many doctors in that town. you know? There was a one-- two Jewish doctors in our town-- three Jewish doctors, actually-- as far as that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was one-- one of the best doctors. And most of his clientele, they were Jewish people. He used to shop himself-- he used to come to our house, drink the soda, because we were the only one who had it-- this business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], Dr. [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: --did he have a role after the Germans came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know really what happened to him, because I think he escaped also. I don't know what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see him in town during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We were saying earlier that you were sent to Zakopane. Can you tell me the circumstances about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zakopane-- when I was in-- working down in the stone quarry-- they used to pick up so many people and went to stone quarry. This was our job. My job, for instance, you know, used to blow out the stones. It was a granite stone, a very hard stone. My job was they gave me a hammer, a small hammer, and I had to break the big pieces for smaller pieces. Every day I had to produce so much. God forbid if I didn't, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to break the stones with the hammer. And we used to use the small stones to build the superhighway we built from Zakopane to Krakow. They were going to go to Germany-- Autobahn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother with you in Zakopane?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his job?

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to work in different places--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the-- in the roads and the highway in our town, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you went to work in Zakopane, did you come home every night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to come once a week, once every two weeks or three weeks sometime. We used to come on Sunday. They took us on a truck. They brought us to town, because it wasn't too far.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to come. We had a chance to go to see our families-- spend at home day, brought our laundry back, dirty laundry, and spend some day. And then evening, you had to go back-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours a day approximately did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we start early in the morning. We start at 7 o'clock in the morning. We work till 5, 6 o'clock in the summertime. Later on, it got darker, so it get a little bit darker. It was a hard work. My hand was like a-- like a-- like a stone, especially my fingers were all knuckled up. You know, you break the stone. You think it's only you break the small pieces. It was a hard work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was supervising this work?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some Polish people and some of the SS troopers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how-- where did you live? Where did you sleep in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We live-- we lived down in a camp. We lived in a home. It was built like a home. They took over the buildings-- in we slept. There were-- there must have been, of us, maybe 40.

speaker: interviewer
text: 40 people from Nowy Targ?

speaker: interviewee
text: 40 to 50 people, yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And a lot-- a lot of people got killed down there for no reason. I'll never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they were killed as a punishment?

speaker: interviewee
text: The punishment, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to shoot them. They used to shoot them. First they beat them, then shoot them, you know? If you don't produce-- where they, you know, for instance, my side to produce so much stone every day. And the food was lousy. It was-- the ration was very poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of foods were they giving you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bread-- bread and the water, and coffee-- you know what I mean-- and soup-- three times a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you working in Zakopane?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zakopane-- I must about two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what changed that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I-- oh, then I was assigned. Then they changed the people. After a while, they brought different people. They'd send us-- go home. We went to different kind of work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after I got through with that, I was sent to-- we used to travel by train, not too far from my home town, another 13 miles-- 13 kilometers-- a small, little village town they called Czarny Dunajec-- capital C-Z-A-R-N-Y dash D-U-N-A-J-E-C.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to work in a-- we used to-- it was like a-- like a mine. We used to mine sand. We used to dig out sand. We used to sort small stone, different-- different size of the stones, then till we come to sand. The company name was the [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a German company.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what were the living conditions like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to travel every day home, back and forth on a train. We start early in the morning. And we work till evening. We got home late night.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were people selected to go to this job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Judenrat, which means-- Judenrat was a Jewish community center. It's not-- I mean, it was a center, that's true. Judenrat-- you know what Judenrat is.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat usually was--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --leadership of a ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were leadership of the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one man was in charge. His name was [NON-ENGLISH], Leon-- [NON-ENGLISH]. He was in charge to the gang labor, to send people to work. My father joined later on the Judenrat, also. My father used to work with the people. For instance, a-- it was a group of us, you know. And my father had to deliver us to the work. Then he went back-- keeping order.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you-- was your family still living in your own home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And there were no changes to the city of Nowy Targ as far as--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no changes until about 1941--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --till the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so when you were at Czarny Dunajec--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dunajec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dunajec-- you were going back to your own home every night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every night I come back to home, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and how--

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked--

speaker: interviewer
text: --long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked down there for about a-- let's see-- for about a year-- for about a year. Then-- then I was assigned, also in Czarny Dunajec, to lumber yard. We used to go. We used to chop the timber. We used to bring, with the horses, the timber to the lumber yard, unload them, cut them. We used to build pre-fab homes. I worked for [NON-ENGLISH]. It's German company-- lumber yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever paid for any of these jobs in the stone quarry, or in the sand mine, or in the lumber mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't received a penny.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And were you compensated in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only compensated for the time I was in concentration camps, which I got very little. And I got a couple hundred dollars. I worked for [NON-ENGLISH] Company in Auschwitz when I was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And their company paid me a little bit--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: --nothing to speak of, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were in these forced labor camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a forced labor camp, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And do you know when you stopped working for Hobug or how long you worked for Hobug?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked-- before I went to Hobug, in between the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] company and Hobug, I worked on the railroad first.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to unload the coal. And we used to lay-- fix the tracks. Wintertime, we used to travel on the steam-- steam locomotive and clean out the snow from the-- so the train-- the train could pass for miles and miles. We used to clean out the snow from the railroad tracks. Then later I was assigned to Hobug. I worked for Hobug like this till August 30, 1942. We used to travel back and forth every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you describe the living conditions at Nowy Targ at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The living condition was very hard, because we had-- we were rationed, our Jewish people. We used to get the-- they used to call [NON-ENGLISH]. And the-- and we were rationed. They used to get so much bread, so much beans, so much sugar, so much butter. It was very hard, because of course, my family, we weren't so bad. Because we had a lot of friends-- farmers-- which we used to deal with them. They used to come to our house. Our house was open Thursday with always market in that town. The farmers used to come. Wintertime, especially, they used to keep themselves warm. We used to sit and drink the soda water and the hot tea. We used to sell, too, hot tea.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had a lot of friends who used to once in a while used to bring us potatoes and the butter. So we had a-- it wasn't so bad. The only thing I remember was a hardship for my parents-- heat up the place. We had no-- we weren't allowed to purchase wood by-- from the-- from the Gentile people, or coal.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I-- as long as I used to work on the railroad station, you know, I used to unload the coal. I used to always wear two pairs of pants, one inside out. I used to wear the knickers. I used to always load up the pants, my pockets, the inside of the knickers, with coal. In my jacket. I used to bring it home. I could hardly walk. We has to bring some coal home. That was tough. It was very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the family have any contact or any news from other parts of Poland at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. No, we didn't get no mail. We didn't-- we didn't correspond. Because we all were together, really, except the neighboring town where my father family was. So we used to hear from them. People used to travel once in a while. And that's where we used to get the news. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know of any of the other activities going on around Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: --well, activities like-- for instance, sometimes on Sunday, we didn't work. So we used to get together. This was more already later on in the ghetto-- when we were in the ghetto. We used to get together, young children, like young kids. We were already-- we were young kids, but we were grow up already, you know, after--

speaker: interviewer
text: When--

speaker: interviewee
text: --all of those years, you know, you become self-sufficient. You grow up very quick.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the ghetto-- when was the ghetto formed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was performed 1941--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And so in 1941, what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all lived-- of course, my family didn't move together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened, we used to trade our homes with our gentile friends. And we were already in process to trade our home. Then it was a little bit too late already. This was 1942. So our family-- there were few-- very few family who lived outside the ghetto yet. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you know what was going on in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Because, I mean, our ghetto was open. It wasn't fenced in like in different cities, you know-- the ghetto was fenced in with the wires, and the bricks, and mortar, and stuff like that. But our ghetto was open. The only difference was all the Jewish people who lived in this part of the section of the town, the Polish people were there to trade their homes. They had to get out, and the Jewish people were moved in. But you-- if you owned your property, you had a chance to trade in your homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was this considered a privilege because your father was in the Judenrat? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know about that. They just-- it was a privilege, because we had a little bit more lean than others, you know? You know, the mayor in our town, and so forth on--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they help us in a way. So we don't have to-- we won't have to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, how can you move? You know, you don't want to leave your home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So were there any other labor camps-- that forced labor camps that you were in after Hobug and--

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while, then those things started. September-- no, August the 30-- finally-- I remember the day before-- August the 29. I came home from my job, from Hobug. This was late at night. Already it was 10 o'clock, because we used to travel by the train. We had to take the last train-- what was-- and we didn't travel in a regular train. We traveled in a caboose, like a regular-- like a freight train for us. It was-- we weren't allowed to-- we weren't allowed to travel with-- with the other people. Jewish people had to be separate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got home. We walk from the station. From our station to my home was, like, maybe three miles. We walk home. And I come home. The whole family was already home. My father was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And besides that, you know, in our neighborhood, the Jewish people used to live in the villages, too. A couple of days before that, they brought all the Jewish people from the small villages to our-- to our town. Our town was a county seat. Let's put it this way. So-- but all the people-- all the Jewish people from the villages-- I had an uncle in Zakopane-- two uncles I had. So him and his wife and two children, then the other uncle, he was a doctor, him and his wife-- they came in. And they all lived in our house. We had 19 people living in our house-- in our little five-room house. So I want you to know how we used to slept. Some sleep in the bed, some slept in the-- on the floor-- on the straw sack. We used to make a-- like a mattress-- a sack filled up with straw. We called it straw sack. And since we're asleep, we slept toe to-- feet to feet-- like that-- 19 people in one house. Then beside that, we had three dogs also. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't imagine it. But it was one harmony. It was pretty nice. What are you going to do? You know. They-- everybody come to our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our house, before the war, as a matter of fact, what they used to call [NON-ENGLISH]. It's a Jewish word. It was an open house. Any poor people, any dignitary came to our town, stay in our house. My parents, they never turned down anybody, never. Before the war, these people used to come to our house. They used to play cards every single night. It was brought-- it had been like this for years, for generations, from my grandfather. Because he had this kind of business years ago, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came to our house and stay over there. And my aunt used to live across the street. They-- she had to get out from the house. She had the two children, my Aunt Mala-- and moved to our house-- my aunt, the older-- my mother's older sister with the two girls. She was one of the professors of the university that was in Dresden.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were grown up people already. They were in their 20's-- late 20s. They all moved to our house. They had to get out. So we lived like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: August the 30-- when I came home the 28th from work, my father came home. It was 10 o'clock. Nobody knew about it, but the people for the-- from the Jewish-- [NON-ENGLISH]. How do you say? Leadership.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leadership. They knew what was going to be. The order came out-- all the Jews must report to the stadium in the morning by 7 o'clock, 8 o'clock in the morning. My father had to leave early, because he had to go gather the Jewish people. He was the councilman in the Jewish community, my dad.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father told my family, everybody, what we have to do. I never forget. My brother was in [NON-ENGLISH] where my-- where my grandfather used to live-- though my mother was, myself, and my sister. When I say-- when I said goodbye to my dad, I never forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father said to me, remember who you are. Never forget where you come from. Never turn down a poor person. I'll never forgot those word. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Say goodbye. You know, kiss me. And it killed everybody. He left home 5 o'clock in the morning. The last time I saw him at the stadium-- and we came to the stadium in the morning-- early morning the next day. My mother took us, me and my sister, and the whole family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to-- we had to walk to the stadium. [CRYING] Excuse me. [CRYING] We had to walk to the stadium. And all of the-- all the Jewish people who was-- who could walk, walk. At the ghetto, there were a lot of Jewish people like that, that were crippled, so they left them behind at home. So walk. We came to the stadium. So there-- we were sorted in different groups. I had seen my father was standing on one side with the Jewish community leaders together. When I came, they put me on the other side-- people from 14 to 50 years old. It meant whoever was healthy. My mother-- they took my mother and my sister on the other side. There were three different groups-- elderly, and the people. And some people, they brought them on the wagons and the wheelbarrow. Because they had to bring them in. They put them on the other side, another group. And our city was right across the railroad station. And we see the cars. The railroad car was standing empty. But we didn't know what it was for. They told us they're going to transport everybody to different camps. We have to leave the town. We're going to go to camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: So me and my friends-- I used to work in the Hobug worker. And before we-- before that, in the Hobug worker, we built a barrack. We didn't know. They had never told us for who, for what. It was prepared for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us in the group where we used to work for the Hobug worker. So we stay on the one side. And whatever we had, possession-- my father gave me his silver watch. He said to me, maybe someday you're going to need it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So meantime, everything we had, we had to give them back to the Germans. But what I did-- the chain from the watch, I hid it. I stuck in my-- underneath my jacket. Much I had to give him back. So I gave him this thing. And they load us up on the truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my mother and my sister waved goodbye to me. And my father still was standing down there. And they took us away back to Czarny Dunajec, to the camp where I worked before. This was my first concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me, did you ever see your father again after that? And where did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was, like I said, he was standing in a different-- a different group. They took all the Jewish community, the leadership, to the Jewish cemetery in our town, on a truck. And all the remaining Jewish people in town, the crippled, older one that couldn't walk-- 500 in all-- they took them to the Jewish cemetery and just stripped them naked. The grave was waiting for them. They had to chase them. The chased them all around the cemetery. They used to beat them, punish them. Finally, they execute them all. And that's where my father--
